considerations.title: Alternative text
considerations.intro: Every <img> needs an alt attribute. Whether it's empty or carries text depends on whether the image conveys content.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Write alt text that conveys the same information as the image, not a literal description of pixels.
sub_considerations.body:
WCAG 2.2 SC 1.1.1 (Non-text Content, Level A) requires that informative images have a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose. "Photo of a man at a desk" is rarely it — what matters is why the image was chosen.

Rule of thumb: read the page with images removed. If the alt text leaves the surrounding paragraph coherent, it's doing its job.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Decorative images take alt="", not a missing attribute and not role="presentation".
sub_considerations.body:
If the image is purely visual decoration — a divider, a flourish, a stock photo that adds nothing the text doesn't already say — the HTML Living Standard expects alt="" so screen readers skip it. A missing alt attribute is different: it tells assistive tech the alternative is unknown, which is worse.

Don't reach for role="presentation" or aria-hidden="true" as substitutes; an empty alt is the canonical signal.
sub_considerations.source_title: HTML Living Standard — Requirements for providing text to act as an alternative for images

considerations.title: Responsive delivery
considerations.intro: Ship the right pixels for the device. Wrong-sized images are the single largest contributor to wasted bytes on most sites.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use srcset + sizes so the browser picks an appropriately sized source, not the largest one.
sub_considerations.body:
Set srcset with width descriptors (image-800.jpg 800w, image-1600.jpg 1600w) and a sizes attribute that tells the browser how wide the image will render at each breakpoint. Without sizes, browsers assume 100vw and may download a 1600w source for a 400px slot.

For art direction (different crops, not just different sizes), reach for <picture> with <source media="..."> instead.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Add loading="lazy" to below-the-fold images; keep above-the-fold ones eager (the default).
sub_considerations.body:
Native lazy loading is a one-attribute win for images outside the initial viewport. It's safe to apply blanket to in-article and grid imagery — browsers handle the threshold heuristics.

Do not lazy-load the LCP image or anything in the hero. Lazy-loading the LCP candidate measurably worsens Core Web Vitals because the request is deferred until layout, then re-prioritised.

considerations.title: Stability & format
considerations.intro: Reserve space before bytes arrive; pick the right codec.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Always set width and height attributes — they let the browser reserve aspect ratio and prevent CLS.
sub_considerations.body:
Modern browsers use the width and height HTML attributes to compute an aspect-ratio box before the image loads, even when CSS resizes the image to width: 100%. Omitting them is the most common cause of Cumulative Layout Shift on content-heavy pages.

Set them to the image's intrinsic pixel dimensions, not its rendered size.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Prefer AVIF then WebP via <picture> with a JPEG/PNG fallback for the long tail of clients.
sub_considerations.body:
AVIF typically saves 30–50% over JPEG at equivalent perceptual quality; WebP saves 20–30%. Both are supported across all evergreen browsers per caniuse. For photographic content, ship AVIF first; for graphics with transparency, WebP usually wins on encoder maturity.

Use <picture><source type="image/avif"><source type="image/webp"><img src="...jpg"></picture> rather than content-negotiating at the CDN — the markup version is cacheable and survives proxies.
